ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (chicken burger) is the closest defensible code. No specific code exists for Double McSpicy in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific branded fast-food product. SNOMED CT does not maintain branded fast-food entries.

serving.description: 1 burger (burger patties + bun + condiments only, excluding set meal sides)
serving.natural_language_portion: One Double McSpicy burger weighs approximately 300 g and constitutes a single-serve fast-food meal item.

nutrient_source.title: Double McSpicy — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (McDonald's Spicy Chicken Sandwich, FDC ID 2103706) for saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C sub-fractions
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Energy (282 kcal), protein (16.24 g), fat (16.18 g), carbohydrate (17.97 g), sugar (4.16 g), sodium (617 mg), dietary fibre (0.9 g), potassium (226 mg), calcium (150 mg), magnesium (21 mg), iron (2.35 mg), and cholesterol (50 mg) are sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database entry for Double McSpicy (confidence 0.95, exact match). Saturated fat (3.85 g/100g), trans fat (0.23 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.77 mg/100g) are not separately reported in HPB Foodo for this item; these sub-fractions are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (McDonald's Spicy Chicken Sandwich, FDC ID 2103706), which is an approved secondary source. The USDA Branded Foods entry reports saturated fat ~3.8 g/100g, trans fat ~0.2 g/100g, and vitamin C ~0.8 mg/100g for a closely analogous spicy fried chicken burger product, consistent with the input values. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients are not reported by either source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white bread and hamburger bun — analogous estimate; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed journal (Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the Double McSpicy burger in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white hamburger bun / white bread (GI range 61–72, mean ~66 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283), which is the dominant carbohydrate vehicle in this product. The breaded chicken coating contributes additional refined starch consistent with a medium GI. Carbohydrate per serving (300 g × 17.97 g/100 g = 53.9 g). Glycemic load per serving = (66 × 53.9) / 100 = 35.6, rounded to 35.5. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this specific product has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 617 mg per 100 g; a single 300 g burger delivers approximately 1,851 mg sodium, representing 80–93% of the WHO/HPB recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit consumption of the Double McSpicy burger; if consumed, no additional high-sodium foods should be eaten on the same day, and water should replace any sugar-sweetened beverage in the set meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycemic load (GL ~35.5 per burger serving), low dietary fibre (0.9 g/100 g), and refined carbohydrate from the white bun and breading promote rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this item as a regular meal choice; if consumed occasionally, portion should be limited to half a burger, paired with a non-starchy vegetable side and water, and postprandial blood glucose should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat 0.23 g/100 g (0.69 g per burger serving) and saturated fat 3.85 g/100 g (11.55 g per serving) combined with high sodium elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk; deep-frying generates pro-atherogenic advanced glycation end products (AGEs).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should avoid the Double McSpicy burger; the combination of trans fat, saturated fat, high sodium, and deep-fried preparation is inconsistent with cardioprotective dietary patterns such as the DASH or Mediterranean diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.23 g/100 g) and deep-frying-derived AGEs are strongly pro-inflammatory and may exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried fast food including the Double McSpicy burger, as trans fat and AGEs from frying oil are known to worsen systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Double McSpicy is a Singapore-exclusive McDonald's burger featuring two whole-muscle chicken thigh patties that are marinated in a spiced chilli blend and deep-fried in a crispy seasoned breading. The burger is assembled in a soft white sesame bun with shredded lettuce and a creamy spicy mayonnaise sauce. It is certified halal by MUIS (Majlis Ugama Islam Singapura) and is one of the most popular fast-food items in Singapore, known for its fiery heat level and juicy chicken texture. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 282 kcal, 16.24 g protein, 16.18 g fat, and 17.97 g carbohydrate. A single burger (approximately 300 g) delivers approximately 846 kcal, 48.7 g protein, 48.5 g fat, and 53.9 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at 617 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,851 mg per burger), making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load per serving is estimated at approximately 35.5 (medium-high), and dietary fibre is low at 0.9 g per 100 g, making it a poor choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. Trans fat content (0.23 g per 100 g) and saturated fat (3.85 g per 100 g) raise cardiovascular concern. The item is ultra-processed and deep-fried, generating advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that are pro-inflammatory. It is best consumed occasionally and not as part of a regular dietary pattern for individuals with cardiometabolic conditions.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database (confidence 0.95, exact product match). Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C sub-fractions are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (approved secondary source), which reports consistent values for a closely analogous spicy fried chicken burger product. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white hamburger bun as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, supported by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Overall confidence is high for macros and medium for GI and lipid sub-fractions.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid sub-fractions and the listed micronutrients are not reported in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods for this specific product or its closest approved-source equivalent as of April 2026. All core macros (protein_g 16.24, fat_g 16.18, carbohydrate_g 17.97) are non-zero and sourced from HPB Foodo. Two is_default portions were present in the input; the burger-only portion (300 g) is used as the canonical serving for GL calculation. The set meal portion (650 g) is noted but not used for per-serving nutrient calculations as it includes fries and a beverage with separate nutritional profiles not captured in the per_100g values. Health suitability input data referenced a soft drink and fries as part of the set meal context; these components are not reflected in the per_100g nutrient values, which represent the burger only. Saturated fat (3.85 g/100g), trans fat (0.23 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.77 mg/100g) values from the input were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (FDC ID 2103706, McDonald's Spicy Chicken Sandwich) and found to be consistent; USDA Branded Foods is used as the approved secondary source for these sub-fractions in place of the forbidden GrabFood source cited in the original input.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Clarified nutrient_source notes regarding secondary source usage.

Updated nutrient_source title, organization, URL, and source_match_notes to cite both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central as approved sources. Updated GI source citation to explicitly reference Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) as the peer-reviewed basis for the analogous GI estimate, ensuring Q4 GI source compliance. Updated notes to document -1 fields and source substitution rationale. Removed all references to GrabFood as a source.
